**New South Wales** (abbreviated as **NSW**) is [Australia's](/wiki/Australia "Australia") most populous [state](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia"), located in the east coast of the continent. It is in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere") between latitudes [28](/wiki/28th_parallel_south "28th parallel south") and [38 degrees south](/wiki/38th_parallel_south "38th parallel south") of the equator and longitudes [141](/wiki/141st_meridian_east "141st meridian east") and [154 degrees east](/wiki/154th_meridian_east "154th meridian east") of the [Universal Prime Meridian](/wiki/Prime_Meridian "Prime Meridian") (formerly known as the [Greenwich meridian](/wiki/Royal_Observatory,_Greenwich "Royal Observatory, Greenwich")). The state is in the warm temperate climatic zone.

## Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Features")]

The area of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") is 801,150 km2 (309,326 sq mi), including 13 km2 (5 sq mi) of island area.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The coastline is 2,137 km (1,328 mi) in length, including 130 km (81 mi) of island coastline.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

[Cape Byron](/wiki/Cape_Byron "Cape Byron"), in the north-east of the state, is Australia's most easterly mainland point.

The state is bordered on the north by [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), on the west by [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia"), and on the south by [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)"). Its coast faces the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea"). New South Wales contains two Federal [enclaves](/wiki/Enclave "Enclave"): the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory") (ACT), and the [Jervis Bay Territory](/wiki/Jervis_Bay_Territory "Jervis Bay Territory").

New South Wales can be divided physically into four sections:

* A thin coastal strip, with climates warming from cool temperate on the far south coast to subtropical near the Queensland border, including the regions south of Sydney such as the [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra "Illawarra") and [Shoalhaven](/wiki/Shoalhaven "Shoalhaven") near [Nowra](/wiki/Nowra "Nowra"). North of Sydney are the [Central Coast](/wiki/Central_Coast,_New_South_Wales "Central Coast, New South Wales") and [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales"), [Hunter Region](/wiki/Hunter_Region "Hunter Region"), [Mid North Coast](/wiki/Mid_North_Coast "Mid North Coast") and [Northern Rivers](/wiki/Northern_Rivers "Northern Rivers") regions.
* The mountainous areas of the [Great Dividing Range](/wiki/Great_Dividing_Range "Great Dividing Range") and the high country surrounding them. Whilst not particularly steep, many peaks rise above 1,000 m, with the highest [Mount Kosciuszko](/wiki/Mount_Kosciuszko "Mount Kosciuszko") at 2,229 m (7,308 ft). This includes the [Southern Highlands](/wiki/Southern_Highlands_(New_South_Wales) "Southern Highlands (New South Wales)"), [Central Tablelands](/wiki/Central_Tablelands "Central Tablelands") and the [Northern Tablelands](/wiki/Northern_Tablelands_(New_South_Wales) "Northern Tablelands (New South Wales)").
* The agricultural plains that fill a significant portion of the state's area, with a much sparser population than the coast, including The [Riverina](/wiki/Riverina "Riverina") area around [Wagga Wagga](/wiki/Wagga_Wagga "Wagga Wagga").
* The Western Plains covering almost two-thirds of the state. In some years production is limited by water availability.

The steep escarpment of the [Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_(Australia) "Blue Mountains (Australia)") to the west of Sydney prevented European exploration beyond the coastal strip for several years until explorer [Gregory Blaxland](/wiki/Gregory_Blaxland "Gregory Blaxland") found a way through in 1813, 25 years after the first settlement in Sydney.

## Cities and towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cities and towns")]

Its four main cities from north to south are [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales"), [Gosford](/wiki/Gosford,_New_South_Wales "Gosford, New South Wales"), [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), and [Wollongong](/wiki/Wollongong,_New_South_Wales "Wollongong, New South Wales") which all lie along the coast. Other cities and towns include [Albury](/wiki/Albury,_New_South_Wales "Albury, New South Wales"), [Broken Hill](/wiki/Broken_Hill,_New_South_Wales "Broken Hill, New South Wales"), [Dubbo](/wiki/Dubbo "Dubbo"), [Tamworth](/wiki/Tamworth,_New_South_Wales "Tamworth, New South Wales"), [Armidale](/wiki/Armidale,_New_South_Wales "Armidale, New South Wales"), [Lismore](/wiki/Lismore,_New_South_Wales "Lismore, New South Wales"), [Nowra](/wiki/Nowra,_New_South_Wales "Nowra, New South Wales"), [Griffith](/wiki/Griffith,_New_South_Wales "Griffith, New South Wales"), [Leeton](/wiki/Leeton,_New_South_Wales "Leeton, New South Wales"), Wagga Wagga, [Goulburn](/wiki/Goulburn,_New_South_Wales "Goulburn, New South Wales"), [Port Macquarie](/wiki/Port_Macquarie,_New_South_Wales "Port Macquarie, New South Wales") and [Coffs Harbour](/wiki/Coffs_Harbour,_New_South_Wales "Coffs Harbour, New South Wales").

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geography_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/New_South_Wales_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-New_South_Wales_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:New_South_Wales_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") in New South Wales

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Autumn_colour_at_Mount_Wilson%2C_New_South_Wales.jpg/250px-Autumn_colour_at_Mount_Wilson%2C_New_South_Wales.jpg)](/wiki/File:Autumn_colour_at_Mount_Wilson,_New_South_Wales.jpg)

Autumn colour at [Mount Wilson](/wiki/Mount_Wilson,_New_South_Wales "Mount Wilson, New South Wales")

Over half of New South Wales has an [arid](/wiki/Arid "Arid") to [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid "Semi-arid") climate, where the rainfall is quite low. Summer temperatures are often very hot, while winter nights can be cold. Most of the rest of the state has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") or an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate"). Precipitation varies throughout the state. The north-west receives the least, getting less than 180 mm (7 in) annually, while most of the east receives between 700 and 1,400 mm (28 and 55 in) of rainfall.[[3]](#cite_note-NSWclimate-3) Rainfall can be as high as 2,000 to 2,500 millimetres (79 to 98 in) in the wettest areas, such as [Charlotte Pass](/wiki/Charlotte_Pass,_New_South_Wales "Charlotte Pass, New South Wales")[[4]](#cite_note-4) and [Dorrigo](/wiki/Dorrigo,_New_South_Wales "Dorrigo, New South Wales"). In the south, precipitation is heaviest in winter because of [cold fronts](/wiki/Cold_front "Cold front") which move across Australia, while in the north, rain is heaviest in summer from tropical systems and even [cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone") on rare occasions.[[3]](#cite_note-NSWclimate-3) During winter, the coastal plain cane be relatively dry because of [foehn winds](/wiki/Southeast_Australian_foehn "Southeast Australian foehn") which blow from the Great Dividing Range.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The mountain can block cold fronts coming from the [Southern Ocean](/wiki/Southern_Ocean "Southern Ocean"), and can produce [foehn winds](/wiki/Foehn_wind "Foehn wind") on the [leeward](/wiki/Leeward "Leeward") side.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The climate in the southern half of the state is generally warm and hot in the summer months and mild and cool in the winter. [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), the largest city, has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (Köppen *Cfa*) with no dry season.[[8]](#cite_note-8) [Wollongong](/wiki/Wollongong "Wollongong") is in the transitional zone between an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") (Köppen *Cfb*) and a humid subtropical climate.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Snowfall can be common in the high-altitude parts of the range, occasionally occurring as far north as the [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") border. On the highest peaks of the [Snowy Mountains](/wiki/Snowy_Mountains "Snowy Mountains"), the climate can be [subpolar oceanic](/wiki/Oceanic_climate#Subpolar_variety_(Cfc,_Cwc) "Oceanic climate"), a [subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") and even an [alpine climate](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate") on the higher peaks with cold temperatures and heavy snowfall. The [Blue Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Mountains_(New_South_Wales) "Blue Mountains (New South Wales)"), [Southern Tablelands](/wiki/Southern_Tablelands "Southern Tablelands") and [Central Tablelands](/wiki/Central_Tablelands "Central Tablelands"), which are situated on the Great Dividing Range, have mild or warm summers and cold winters, although not as cold as those in the Snowy Mountains.[[3]](#cite_note-NSWclimate-3)

The highest maximum temperature recorded was 49.7 °C (121 °F) at [Menindee](/wiki/Menindee,_New_South_Wales "Menindee, New South Wales") on 10 January 1939 and at [Bourke](/wiki/Bourke,_New_South_Wales "Bourke, New South Wales").[[10]](#cite_note-Extremes-10) The lowest minimum temperature ever recorded was −23.0 °C (−9 °F) at [Charlotte Pass](/wiki/Charlotte_Pass,_New_South_Wales "Charlotte Pass, New South Wales") in the Snowy Mountains on 29 June 1994. This is also the lowest temperature recorded in the whole of Australia.[[10]](#cite_note-Extremes-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Charlotte Pass often has a snow depth of 2 metres in winter,[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) and snow has even accumulated and persisted in the middle of summer.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)
